channel.title: Deep-Learning on Roverdi's Website
channel.description: Recent content in Deep-Learning on Roverdi's Website

item.title: Multilayer Perceptron 1: Structure and Feedforward
item.description:
In the previous post, we explored the Perceptron: we saw that it can learn and make simple decisions, but we also discovered it has a hard limit. Being a linear classifier, it can only solve problems where the data can be separated by a straight line.

The most iconic limitation in the history of neural networks was the logical XOR (Exclusive OR) operator. Since the points of that problem can’t be split by a single line, a lone Perceptron loops forever and never learns the rule.

item.title: Perceptron: The Foundation of Deep Learning
item.description:
At its core, all modern artificial intelligence is just trying to imitate what you do without thinking.

When you decide whether to cross the street, or whether that email belongs in the spam folder, your brain is processing signals, weighing evidence, and making a simple decision: yes or no.

In artificial neural networks, everything started as an attempt to replicate that behavior in a mathematical model, giving birth to what we know as the Perceptron — still, to this day, one of the most elegant ideas in computing.
